generations.text:
**To my beloved daughter,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast at the window, and the sea roars as if it were the voice of God Himself, demanding that I tell you something. But I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as the night falls and the storm draws near, I find myself compelled to reach out once more.

You may think me a fool, but I have never stopped thinking of you. The days have passed like the tides, never still, always shifting, and in the quiet of the lighthouse, when the world is hushed and the sea is still, I find myself remembering your laughter, your wide eyes, and the way you once used to come to me and ask, "What is the light, Papa?" and I would say, "It is the light of hope, my little one, the light that guides the lost, and the light that keeps the dark at bay."

I have been a poor father, I know. I let the sea take me, and I let the years pass, and I allowed my heart to grow cold. I told myself that the distance was for your good, that you would be better off without me. But I was wrong. I have been wrong. I have been a coward.

I have no excuse for the silence, no justification for the years I have spent in the arms of the lighthouse, watching the world pass me by. I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that aches with the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since the last time our eyes met, and though the world has turned many times upon its axis, I find myself still bound by the silence between us.

You were a child when you left, and I, a man too stubborn to say the words that might have kept you. I told myself that distance was a gift, that time would mend what words could not. But time, my dear, is a cruel and fickle thing. It does not mend, only erodes.

Tonight, the sea howls with a fury I have not seen in decades. The storm is coming—great and terrible, as though the very heavens are weeping. I have seen the clouds darken, the wind scream like a beast in pain, and I fear that this night may be my last. I have spent my life standing on this lonely rock, watching the sea, and now I find myself watching the end of it all.

I have no regrets, not truly, but I do regret the way I let you go. I regret the silence, the absence, the wounds left unhealed. I regret that I did not reach for you when you were still within reach. I regret that I was too afraid to ask for your forgiveness, too proud to beg for your love.

If I do not survive this night, know this: I

**The Lighthouse, Black崖 Point**  
*April 12th, 1897*

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence. The sea howls outside like a beast in torment, and the wind howls with it, as if the very elements themselves are crying out. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to write, not to convince you to come back, but to tell you that I have never stopped thinking of you.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for over thirty years, and I have seen many storms. I have weathered tempests that would have broken lesser men, and I have watched the sea rise like a vengeful spirit, swallowing ships whole. But never have I felt so alone as I do now, with the storm outside and the silence within.

You remember the day you left. You were young, and you thought you were right to do so. I do not blame you for that. I was not the father you needed, and I was not the man you deserved. I was too busy with the light, too consumed by duty, to see the pain you carried. I was a man who saw the world through glass and flame, not through the eyes of a child.

I have thought of you every night, even when I could not speak your name. I have

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not. I have been meaning to write for some time now, but the weight of my words has always seemed heavier than the storm that now threatens to consume us all.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I have not seen you, nor heard your voice, in all that time. I have kept the lighthouse, as I always have, with the same steadfast devotion, yet the silence of this house has grown more oppressive with each passing year. I used to think that time would soften the edges of our quarrel, but it has only sharpened them. I have no excuse for the way I left you, and for that, I am truly sorry. I have spent many nights staring into the abyss of the sea, wondering if I might have done something—anything—to have spared you the pain.

Tonight, the wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with a fury that has not been seen in decades. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and I have told myself that I must remain here, for it is my duty. But I do not know if I will survive the night. The old bones of this lighthouse are not as strong as they once were, and the storm shows no sign of abating. I have heard the tales of those who have perished in such tempests, and I

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a quill that trembles more from the weight of my heart than from the cold wind that howls through the lighthouse’s cracks. The sea roars like a beast in torment, and the sky is black as the abyss. I’ve lit the beacon, as I always do, though I fear it may be the last time I can. The storm is coming, and I do not know if I shall see dawn.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, silent years. I have not heard your voice, nor seen your face. You left like a ghost, and I have been left to wander the lonely rocks with only the wind for company. I have tried to write to you, but your address has long since changed, and the postman has grown tired of my letters. I suppose you have forgotten me, as I have forgotten how to be a father.

But tonight is different. Tonight, the sea is restless, and the stars are hidden behind a veil of storm. I have been thinking of you, and I have finally found the courage to write, not as a man who has been wronged, but as a father who has been lost.

You were once the light in my life, Eliza. Before the sea took your mother, before the world turned its back on us, you were my light. I watched you grow, and I watched you leave. I did not understand, and I did

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. The wind howls like a beast outside, and the sea roars as though it seeks to swallow the land whole. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to write, not to beg for your forgiveness, but to say what I have long kept buried beneath the silence.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child who believed the world was a place of endless possibilities, and that I — your father — was a man who had forgotten how to love. I was wrong. I was a fool. I let pride and stubbornness harden my heart, and in doing so, I lost the one thing that mattered most: you.

I have kept the lighthouse, not for the glory, but for the quiet, the solitude, the chance to remember. The sea has always been a cruel mistress, and I have been her servant for more than twenty years. It is a life of duty, of duty alone. But tonight, as I look out upon the darkening horizon, I feel the weight of my years pressing down upon me. The storm is coming, and I fear I may not see the dawn.

I do not know if I will survive this night. I do not know if I will be found, or if the sea will take me in its cold embrace

**April 12th, 1892**  
**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse, rattling the windows and shaking the very walls of this old stone fortress. The sea, which has always been my companion, now roars with fury, as though it too mourns the silence between us.

Five years have passed since I last saw you, and yet it feels like a lifetime. I’ve tried to forget the day you left—how you stood at the door, your face pale, your eyes full of sorrow, and said, “You don’t understand, Father.” I believe that now. I do not understand. Not then, not now.

I’ve kept the lighthouse, as you would have wanted. I’ve tended the light, as you once asked me to, and I’ve watched the sea for years, hoping to see your ship return. But it never did. And I have not seen you since.

I know you blame me for what happened. I know you think I was too strict, too distant, too much of a man of the sea and not of the heart. But I only wanted to protect you. I wanted to give you a life of comfort, of safety, of something more than the sea and its merciless tides. And yet, you chose your own path, and I chose mine.

Tonight, the storm is

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a beast at the door, the sea roiling in fury, and the light within the tower flickering as if it too is afraid. Five years have passed since you last set foot in this place, and yet the weight of your absence still sits heavy upon my heart like the stones of the foundation beneath me.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. Not a single word. Not a letter. I suppose I did not expect you to return, and I did not expect you to write. But now, as the storm gathers strength and the waves claw at the cliffs with a desperation that mirrors my own, I find myself writing this, not to beg for your forgiveness, but to tell you the truth—because I fear I may not live to see another dawn.

You were a child when you left, and I was a man who believed that the sea was a thing of beauty, a thing of purpose. I believed that the light I kept burning was more than a beacon—it was a promise, a duty, a legacy. But I was wrong, Eleanor. I was wrong to believe that the world would not change, that the heart of a man could remain unshaken by the storms of life. I was wrong to think that you would stay.

I do not know what drove you away. I do not know if it was the cruel words I spoke, or the silence I

*Dearest Eleanor,*

*If you are reading this, then I have either perished in the storm or—God forbid—have finally found the courage to reach out to you. Either way, I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not. I have not written to you in five years, and I have not spoken to you in years longer. But tonight, as the sky darkens and the wind howls like a banshee, I feel the weight of silence pressing upon me like the very sea that I have spent my life guarding.*

*You were once my light, Eleanor. Not the light of the lighthouse, but the light of my heart. I remember the day you left, the way your eyes held both sorrow and defiance as you stepped onto that carriage. You said you needed to find your own path, and I, in my foolish pride, allowed you to go. I told myself it was for the best. That you would grow, that you would find happiness, that I would be a father who let you go. But I was wrong. I was wrong to let you go. I was wrong to let the silence grow between us.*

*This storm is unlike any I have seen in my years here. The waves are rising, the wind is howling, and I feel the old bones in my body tremble with fear. I have always been the keeper of this light, the one who watches over the sea, but tonight

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a vengeful spirit outside this lighthouse, and I can feel the sea clawing at the rocks below. I do not know if I shall see another dawn. But if I do, I must tell you this: I have never stopped thinking of you.

Five long years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I have been a fool to let pride and anger keep me from reaching out. I have spent those years watching the waves, the stars, and the endless horizon, but I have never seen you as clearly as I do now. You were never the cause of our parting, and I know that now. It was I who turned away, who let the bitterness of our last argument drive me to silence.

I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness, of routine. You know this. But I have not been able to bear the thought of you, of the life you were meant to have, and of the choices I could not make for you. I was afraid of what I might say, of what I might do. And so I let the silence grow, and it grew into a chasm that neither time nor distance could bridge.

But tonight, as the storm gathers, I am not the man I once was. I have seen the sea in its fury, and I have seen it in its

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a quill that trembles not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since the last time you set foot in this lighthouse, and five years have not dimmed the ache of your absence. I have kept the lamp burning, as I always have, but I fear now that even its light may not be enough to guide me through the storm that is coming.

The wind has been howling all night, like a wounded beast, and the sea, that ever-hungry thing, has grown restless. I have seen the clouds gather like a black shroud over the horizon, and I feel the old bones in my joints groan with the strain of it all. I do not know if I shall see dawn.

But I must write, for I have something to say—something I have kept locked in my chest longer than the lantern's oil. You were once the light of my life, Eleanor. Not the lamp, but you. And though I have been cruel, and though I have turned my back, I have never stopped longing for the day when you might return.

I know you have your own life now—your own burdens, your own choices. I do not blame you. I have not blamed you. I have only ever blamed myself. I was a fool to let pride and fear drive the wedge between us. I was a lighthouse keeper, not a father

**Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and though the world has turned, I fear I have not. The sea, which once brought me peace, now roars with a fury I have never known. Tonight, a storm is coming—one that may not spare this old lighthouse or the man who calls it home.

I have not written to you, nor have I sent word, for I feared the words might not be enough. I feared they might bring you more pain than comfort. But now, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the waves crash against the cliffs with a vengeance, I find myself longing for the sound of your voice. For the first time in years, I feel the weight of my silence, and I know I must speak.

You were my light, Eliza. Not the light of the lighthouse, though it was my duty to keep it burning, but the light that once guided my days. I remember the way you would stand by the window, watching the sea, your eyes wide with wonder. You used to ask me questions about the stars and the tides, and I would tell you stories until the sun rose. Those were the days when I believed I could protect you from the world’s cruel winds.

But the world has a way of changing, and so did I. I was not the man you thought I

**Lighthouse Keeper’s Letter to My Daughter**  
**17th of October, 1889**  
*Porthmore Head, Isle of Orkney*

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a trembling hand, for the wind howls like the wailing of the dead and the sea, that old, unrelenting beast, is rising in fury. The waves have grown so tall that even the strongest of the lighthouse’s timbers shudder beneath their weight. I have spent many a night in this tower, but never have I felt so alone. And now, with the storm upon us, I find myself thinking of you—my daughter, my dear Eleanor—more than ever.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since the last time we spoke. Five years since you left, with your father’s heart broken and your mother’s grave by my side. I have not written, not even a single line, since the day you shut the door in my face, and I did not ask for forgiveness. I could not, not then. I had no right.

But now, as I stand before the great beacon, its light still burning through the darkness, I find that I have no choice. This storm may take me, and if it does, I would not want the last words I speak to be the silence of a man who has lost everything.

You were never the daughter I wanted. I had dreams of you being a proper lady

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very stones of this lighthouse whole. I have stood watch through many storms, but never have I felt so alone as I do tonight. The lantern flickers, and I pray it will hold until the dawn.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left this place, and me, behind. I do not know if you still remember the scent of the sea, or the sound of the waves against the rocks. I do not know if you remember the lighthouse at all. But I do know that you were once my light, and now I am left in the dark.

I have not spoken to you in so long, and I fear I may never again. But tonight, as the storm gathers, I feel the need to say these words, even if they fall unheard. You were never cruel, Eleanor. You were never unkind. You were just... lost. And I, in my pride, let you go.

I have seen the world beyond this coast, Eleanor. I have read the letters you sent, and I have read the ones you did not. I have heard the stories of your life, of your choices, of your regrets. And I have wondered, night after night, if I could have done something different. If I could have been

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and yet the weight of that absence still clings to me like the salt of the sea. I have not spoken to you, and I fear I may never again. But tonight, as the wind howls like a beast and the sea roars with fury, I feel the need to speak — not to you, but to the ghost of the daughter I once knew.

The storm is coming, and I do not know if I shall see dawn. The lantern has not been lit for days, and the wind has grown so fierce that even the strongest of men would falter. I have been here all these years, alone, tending to the light, watching over the waves, and yet I have not been watching over you.

You were a child when you left, and I did not understand the fire in your eyes. You spoke of a future beyond the cliffs, beyond the sea, beyond me. I did not understand, and I did not ask. I thought, perhaps, that you would return, that time would mend the wounds we had made. But time does not heal wounds, only bury them beneath layers of dust and regret.

I have heard stories of you, of your life in the city, of the work you do in the world beyond these shores. I have heard whispers of your

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in the company of those who love you, and that the cold winds of this cruel world do not reach you as they do me. Five years have passed since I last set pen to paper, and yet it feels like only yesterday that I stood at the edge of the cliff, watching the sea with a heart both heavy and aching. You were there, too—though not in the way I wish you had been.

I have not written to you, not truly, because I feared the words would not carry the weight of what I had to say. And yet, as the storm brews on the horizon, I find myself with no choice but to speak. The sea is restless tonight, and I fear the light may not hold. I fear I may not hold.

You were always my light, Eleanor. Even when I did not know how to be a father, you were the one who reminded me that love is not always gentle, and not always kind. You were the first to understand that the sea is not a place of comfort, but of duty. You once said to me, "Duty is a cruel mistress, but it is the only one I know." I remember that now, and I am sorry.

I have not been a good father. I have not been a good man. I have let the loneliness of this place, the silence of the night, and the weight of the world grow too

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lighthouse, and the sea roars with a fury that seems to echo the storm of my soul. I do not know if I will see the dawn, but if I do, I must speak these words before they are lost to time and regret.

Five years have passed since you last set foot in this place, and five years have felt like a lifetime of silence. I have not spoken your name in all that time, not even to myself. Yet now, as the tempest gathers strength and the light of the lighthouse flickers uncertainly, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, to the daughter I once held close, and to the mother I failed.

You were but a child when you left, Eliza. A child with eyes like the sea and a heart full of dreams. I watched you grow, and though I tried, I could not keep you. You were too bright, too full of fire, and I—ah, I was too afraid to let you go. I feared the world would take you, just as it took your mother. I feared I would lose you again. And so I held you back, not with love, but with fear.

But fear is a cruel master. It does not protect, only binds. And in my fear, I

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 3rd, 1887**

**Lighthouse, Cliffton Point**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. It has been five long years since you left this house, and five years since I have spoken your name without the weight of regret pressing upon me. Tonight, as the wind howls across the cliffs and the sea roars like a beast in torment, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in all that time.

The storm is coming, Eleanor. Not just a storm of wind and rain, but one that may take us all. I have seen the sky darken with an ominous purple, and the sea, which has been calm for weeks, now churns with fury. The lighthouse light must remain burning, as it always has, to guide those who might be lost in this chaos. But I fear it may not be enough to save me.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. Not the light of the lighthouse, but the light of your laughter, your kindness, and the way you looked at the world with such wonder. I remember the day you first came to visit, with your books and your dreams of the sea. You were full of life, full of hope. I was a man who had long since lost his way, and you, my dear, were the one who reminded

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this by the flickering light of the oil lamp, its glow like the last ember of a dying fire. The sea howls outside, a sound that has haunted me for years—just as it has haunted me since the day I last saw you. Five long years, Eleanor. Five years since you left, and five years since I've heard your voice. I have not spoken to you in five years, and now, as the storm gathers strength and the wind screams like a banshee, I find myself writing to you—perhaps for the last time.

You were not the first to leave me, nor will you be the last. But you were the only one who ever truly understood me. You were my light in the darkness, my reason for keeping this lighthouse lit, for standing alone on this rock, watching the world pass by in a blur of waves and stars. You were my daughter, and I was your father. And yet, I let you go.

I do not know why you left. You never said. You only sent a letter, brief and cold, telling me that you could not stay. That you had to go. That you needed to be free. I read it over and over, trying to find the words that would bring me back to you. But there were none. Only silence. And so I let it go, as I let you go.

But now, as the storm approaches, I find

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit by the fire, the wind howling like a banshee outside the lighthouse, and I find myself compelled to write. The sea roars in protest, as if it too remembers the days when you still came to visit, when we sat by the light and shared stories of the world beyond this rock. But now, five years have passed since the last time I saw your face, and the silence between us has grown as deep and unyielding as the ocean itself.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. Not a word. Not a letter. I suppose I thought time would mend what words could not. But the years have only hardened the wounds, and the silence has become a thing of its own, heavy and cold as the stone walls of this tower.

The storm is coming. A terrible one. The sky is black as ink, the sea a beast in a rage, and the wind carries with it the sound of thunder that shakes the very bones of this lighthouse. I have seen storms like this before, and I know they are not to be trifled with. The waves are rising, and the light must shine. It is my duty, as it has been for thirty years. But I do not know if I will see the dawn.

I have always believed in the light, Eleanor. Not just the light of the beacon, but the light of hope, of truth, of love. And

*Dearest Eliza,*

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse. The sea is restless, and the sky is black as a funeral shroud. I fear this night may be my last. Yet, I must tell you what has been on my heart for five long, cruel years.

I have not spoken to you, not in words, not in letters—though I have penned many. I have not seen your face, not heard your voice, not held you in my arms. And yet, I think of you every night as the light burns through the storm. You were once the light in my life, Eliza. Now, it seems, I have only darkness.

I do not know if you still remember the time you came to visit, when you were but a child. You stood on the cliff, wide-eyed and curious, and asked me if the sea ever cried. I told you it did, in the way it roared and crashed against the rocks. You believed me, and for a moment, I thought I saw the same light in your eyes that I once saw in your mother’s. But then you left, and the light faded.

I have tried, Eliza. I have tried to forgive, to forget, to move on. But the sea is a cruel mistress, and so is memory. I have spent every day since your departure watching the horizon, waiting for a sign, a letter

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that aches more than the wind that howls against these ancient stones. Five years have passed since you left, and yet the silence between us has only grown colder. I have not spoken your name aloud in all that time, and now, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I find myself wondering—what if I do not survive to see the morning?

You were once my light, Eliza. Not the light of the lighthouse, but the light that kept me from drowning in the darkness of my own regrets. I used to tell myself that I was merely a keeper of the beacon, but I was wrong. I was a keeper of you. And now, I fear, I have failed you.

I do not know what drove you away. I do not know if it was the cruel words I spoke, the silence I kept, or the way I let the sea and time wear me down. I have tried to find the words to apologize, but they have always slipped through my fingers like sand. I have no excuse, only the truth: I was broken, and I did not know how to mend myself, let alone you.

The storm is upon us now. The wind howls like a beast in pain, and the sea roars as if it too is weeping. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and I wonder if it will be the last

**April 12th, 1887**  
**The Lighthouse, Point Dune, Cornwall**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The sea howls tonight, as if it knows what I must say. The wind screams like a child in distress, and the waves lash the cliffs with a fury that mirrors the tempest within me. I fear the storm may claim me before the dawn, and so I must speak, for I know I may never have the chance again.

Five years have passed since you left. Five long, bitter years. I have watched the sun rise and set, and with each passing day, the weight of your absence has grown heavier. I have kept the light burning, as I have always done, but now it seems the light is but a reflection of the darkness that has settled in my soul.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. I remember the day you were born as if it were yesterday—your tiny fingers grasping mine, your eyes wide with wonder at the world. You were my joy, my pride, my everything. But something changed. You grew distant, and I did not understand. You told me you were leaving, that you could not stay. I did not argue. I did not try to hold you back. I thought perhaps, in time, you would come back.

But you did not.

I have tried to forgive you

**Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the windowpane. The sea roars in defiance, and the sky is a swirling tempest of black and silver. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as I sit here, the weight of silence presses upon me like the very storm outside.

You were twelve when you left, and I did not stop you. I thought I was doing what was right, what was necessary. I thought you would understand. But you did not. And I have not. We have not. Five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years of silence, of letters left unopened, of memories that haunt me like the ghosts of the dead.

I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness. You know this. I have stood here, night after night, through the harshest storms, the darkest nights, the most cruel winds. I have kept the light burning, not for glory, but for the safety of those who sail upon these waters. But now, as I face this storm that may claim me, I find myself wondering if I have ever truly kept the light for anyone but myself.

You were my light, Eliza. You were the reason I kept going, the reason I believed in something more than the cold, unyielding sea. And yet, I let you go. I let you believe I had no room

**The Lighthouse at Blackrock**

**27th of April, 1893**

*To My Dearest Eliza,*

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast in the distance, the sea roiling as though it knows the hour of our reckoning. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see the dawn.

I have not seen you in five years, Eliza. Five years, and yet it feels like an eternity. I have not written, not spoken, not even dared to dream of the daughter who once came to me with eyes like starlight and a heart that beat with the rhythm of the sea.

You were the light, Eliza. The light that once shone from the top of this lighthouse, guiding the ships safe to shore, and in you, I found the same light. But somewhere along the way, that light faded. And I—like the lighthouse—was left standing, alone, in the dark.

I do not know what led to the rift between us. You left, and you never looked back. I tried to reach you, to call out, to write, but your silence was a wall, and I had no key to break it. Perhaps you were right to leave, and perhaps I was wrong to hold on. I do not know. All I know is that I have spent these years as a man without a daughter, and a father without

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. It has been five long, cruel years since the last time we spoke, and I fear the storm that now roils the skies may be the final thing to break me. Yet I must write—must tell you what I have not dared to say in all this time.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child with fire in your eyes and a heart too big for the world. I tried to hold you close, but you were never meant to stay. You were meant to find your own path, to chase the horizon like the light I keep burning through the night. And I, in my foolishness, let you go.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I will live to see the dawn of the next. **The Lighthouse, Blackthorn Point**

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with the wind howling like a wounded beast outside, the sea roaring as if it would tear the very foundations of this stone from the earth. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than just the lighthouse.

I have always been a man of duty, of duty to the light, to the sea, to the souls who depend on my watchful eye. **To My Dearest Eleanor,**

It only deepened the wound.
